data.title: Tennessee Valley Authority to develop commercial fusion plant at retired coat site, Axios reports
data.description: The Tennessee Valley Authority plans to use Type One Energy technology at a retired coal plant, which will be the site of a commercial fusion plant, Axios reported quoting Type One Energy CEO Christofer Mowry.

data.title: Trump Says U.S. and China Approve TikTok Deal After Call With Xi — WSJ
data.description:
By Amrith Ramkumar, Alex Leary and Lingling Wei

WASHINGTON — President Trump announced Friday that he and Chinese leader Xi Jinping approved an agreement for a group of investors to take control of TikTok's U.S. operations, capping off months of tense negotiations that put the video-sharing app at the center of trade talks between the two sides.

"I just completed a very productive call with President Xi of China. We made progress on many very important issues including trade, fentanyl, the need to bring the war between Russia and Ukraine to an end, and the approval of the TikTok deal," he said on his Truth Social platform.

Trump said he and Xi agreed to meet at the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation summit in South Korea this fall and that he would travel to China early next year, and that Xi would reciprocate. "The call was a very good one, we will be speaking again by phone, appreciate the TikTok approval, and both look forward to meeting at APEC!" Trump said.

Negotiators from both sides discussed the agreement during trade talks in Madrid earlier this week, then needed Trump and Xi to sign off to finalize it.

A spokeswoman for TikTok didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Xi appeared to support a commercial solution to TikTok while asserting China's legal authority over the app's core technology, according to the official Xinhua News Agency.

"The Chinese government respects the wishes of the company and is pleased to see businesses conduct commercial negotiations based on market rules to reach a solution that complies with Chinese law and regulations while balancing the interests of all parties," Xi was quoted by Xinhua as saying.

The Chinese leader also expressed his hope that the U.S. would provide an "open, fair, and non-discriminatory business environment" for Chinese companies investing in the U.S.

Write to Amrith Ramkumar at amrith.ramkumar@wsj.com, Alex Leary at alex.leary@wsj.com and Lingling Wei at Lingling.Wei@wsj.com

data.title: Nike Is Maintained at Neutral by UBS
'Stan and Gus' Review: Monsters and Monuments
data.description:
By Christoph Irmscher

They were as different as could be and yet became fast friends. Augustus "Gus" Saint-Gaudens (1848-1907), the son of a French-born shoemaker, was introverted, moody and filled with self-doubt, moving in geological time when finishing his sculptures. Stanford "Stan" White (1853-1906), the scion of a well-connected New York family, was flamboyant, loud and constantly on the move (his office had no chair), gripped by an insatiable appetite for new architectural projects catering to the needs of the rich.

As Henry Wiencek points out in "Stan and Gus: Art, Ardor, and the Friendship That Built the Gilded Age," White left his mark on late-19th-century New York City, racking up commissions for his firm, McKim, Mead & White, the way others collect postage stamps: the Judson Memorial Church on the south side of Washington Square, the Washington Arch on the north side, the Century Association in Midtown, the Players club in Gramercy, the Metropolitan Club on the Upper East Side, the Tiffany building on Fifth Avenue. White brought the European Renaissance to Manhattan, sometimes literally. For one of his masterpieces, the lavish William C. Whitney mansion at 871 Fifth Ave., he imported gilt-iron and bronze gates from the Palazzo Doria Pamphilj in Rome.

The friendship between the two men allegedly began in 1875 when White, walking near New York City's Union Square, heard someone sing a theme from "Don Giovanni," Mozart's opera about an amoral rake very much like White himself. When White ran up to the apartment where the music was coming from, he found Saint-Gaudens.

For all his swagger in public, White was, Mr. Wiencek tells us, surprisingly solicitous of Saint-Gaudens, defending him against impatient clients, cheering him on but also putting him in his place when necessary: "You OrangOtang," White chided his boorish friend when the latter suddenly required an unrealistically large block of marble (9 feet high and 7 feet wide and deep) for the — of course long-delayed — family tomb of Edwin Morgan, a former governor of New York. (Saint-Gaudens got his wish.)

White was an aficionado of surfaces, a virtuoso of shiny excess. His redesign of the Seventh Regiment Armory reception room on Park Avenue featured Tiffany tiles, oak floors and walls, chandeliers with multiple gaslights, as well as columns wrapped with chains covered in faux rust. A planner of party spaces — the ballroom he added to the Whitney mansion was likely New York's largest — he was an inveterate partyer himself. But he could also be a sensitive collaborator, ready to adjust his thunderous temperament to his friend's quieter artistic proclivities. White's exedra (a base or platform) for Saint-Gaudens's statue of Adm. David Farragut (1881) in New York's Madison Square Park perfectly complements the sculptor's work, which shows Farragut in action, clutching a pair of binoculars as an invisible breeze blows open the front of his coat. White's stone base picks up on the theme of movement, picturing wind-tossed waves beneath Farragut and dolphins at either end. This fusion of sensibilities extended, Mr. Wiencek writes, to the artists' personal lives: It seems that Saint-Gaudens and White, at least for a time, became lovers.

Of the two men, Saint-Gaudens was the more reflective. One of his most intense projects was the 1891 statue for the tomb of Marian "Clover" Hooper Adams in Rock Creek Cemetery in Washington, D.C. Married to Henry Adams, the historian and writer, Clover had died by suicide following a long struggle with mental illness. After the usual delays, Saint-Gaudens delivered a large, enigmatic, hooded figure seated on a rough stone bench fashioned by White. As Mr. Wiencek relates, Adams, who had commissioned the work, returned to his wife's grave almost daily, convinced that the statue spoke to him, its face bearing an expression "almost amounting to defiance." That reaction will be familiar to visitors to Saint-Gaudens's 1897 memorial to Robert Gould Shaw and the Massachusetts 54th Regiment at the edge of Boston Common. A magnificent bas-relief tribute to one of the Civil War's first African-American units, the monument sticks, as the poet Robert Lowell observes, like "a fishbone / in the city's throat." The faces of Col. Shaw's soldiers are rendered with such empathetic precision that one can still sense their determination today.

Mr. Wiencek, a historian and the author of "Master of the Mountain: Thomas Jefferson and His Slaves" (2012), doesn't gloss over White's brutality. A serial abuser of young women, White was 47 when he likely drugged and raped the 16-year-old Evelyn Nesbit, an aspiring actress. Harry Kendall Thaw, who would marry the traumatized woman five years later, turned out to be scarcely better. A vengeful bully, he had some of Evelyn's teeth pulled out when he learned that White had paid to fix them. Beside himself with jealousy, Thaw finally shot and killed White during the performance of a musical at Madison Square Garden. Over the course of the two ensuing trials, public sentiment turned against the victim. Saint-Gaudens, who had a son with one of his models and worried that his own affairs might come to light, kept mostly mum. Cancer claimed him before Thaw was found not guilty by reason of insanity.

While Mr. Wiencek doesn't make any apologies for White, the absence of any larger evaluative framework in this book leaves many questions unanswered. Does Saint-Gaudens redeem himself through his art in ways the more seriously compromised White couldn't? Are White's misdeeds the flip side of an aesthetic that relentlessly prizes things over people?

Perhaps some poetic justice resides in the fact that if Saint-Gaudens's memorials still mesmerize today, some of White's most cherished creations — the Whitney mansion, the Herald Building — have since been razed. Those who walk up to the Farragut memorial for a closer look at the pebble-strewn ground before it (also designed by White) will find a bronze crab with the names of both artists engraved on its shell. A distinctly uncuddly creature, that metal crustacean may indeed be a fitting symbol for this complicated friendship.

  • Mr. Irmscher is the author of "Max Eastman: A Life" and other books.

data.title: Argentina central bank leans on dollar reserves to curb peso weakness
data.description:
Argentina's peso was subdued on Friday after the central bank used reserves to manage liquidity at the top of its floating exchange rate band, aiming to curb strong dollar demand from institutional investors wary of political instability.

The currency hovered at historic lows, underscoring deepening investor skepticism ahead of pivotal midterm elections in October that could strip libertarian President Javier Milei of crucial congressional support for his reform agenda.

"We're going to sell every last dollar (of the Central Bank's reserves) at the top of the band," Economy Minister Luis Caputo said Thursday night during a streaming interview.

By 1500 GMT, the wholesale peso was trading at 1,475 per dollar , near the band's ceiling of 1,475.32, while Argentina's country risk reached 1,400 basis points, its highest in a year.

Argentina's central bank has sold $432 million over the past two days, resuming interventions last seen in mid-April after a $20 billion International Monetary Fund deal that initially loosened foreign exchange curbs.

Since April, the exchange rate has operated within a daily floating band. The central bank must step in when the currency hits the upper or lower limits.

"Given that this pace of dollar sales by the central bank would imply a loss of reserves of approximately $10 billion before the elections — approximately 70% of the funds already disbursed by the IMF — the likelihood of abandoning the exchange rate band scheme before the October 26 elections increases," BancTrust & Co said.

While the central bank's international reserves stand at $39.407 billion, analysts say provisional official data indicates that net foreign exchange reserves for intervention are estimated at just $6 billion.

Continued dollar sales, experts warn, risk accelerating reserve depletion, jeopardizing near-term debt payments and triggering a rise in bond issuance to bridge funding gaps.

Caputo affirmed the current exchange rate framework and said the Treasury is working to ensure January debt payments, promising to provide clarity to bondholders once finalized.

The Buenos Aires stock market, with its leading index, the S&P Merval , was down 1.28%. The index has lost 14% this month and 32.6% year-to-date.

Argentines vote in October to renew part of the lower house and Senate, amid uncertainty over how the government will recover from setbacks in Buenos Aires province in recent contests.

data.title: Canada Retail Sales Look to Recover after Falling 0.8% in July — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canadians are still shopping even as the economy stutters and job losses mount, with an early tally of receipts pointing to a rebound in retail sales last month.

Statistics Canada estimated sales rose 1.0% from the month before in August, retracing a 0.8% drop for retailers in July.

Even if retail volumes don't grow as strongly for the latest month, given a rise in goods prices, it still indicates a tailwind for the economy and hints at some resilience in household spending that was a bright spot for an economy that languished in the second quarter.

The data agency offered no details with the forecast for last month, which was based on responses from roughly half of retailers surveyed and will be revised. The fall in July sales was in line with the agency's earlier advance estimate and the consensus forecast of economists.

Expectations are high the uncertainty caused by the trade conflict with the U.S. and weakness in the labor market will weigh on consumption, but early fears tariffs could throw Canada into a recession have faded and the fallout from the Trump administration's policies and tariffs have so far been largely concentrated in trade-exposed industries. Canadians also have shunned travel to the U.S. this year, offering hope staycations will boost local spending.

"The sharp decline in retail sales in July is not as bad as it first looks, with August's flash estimate pointing to a swift rebound, suggesting the weakness was mostly payback for earlier strength rather than a sign that households are pulling back in response to a softening jobs market," said Thomas Ryan, North America economist at research firm Capital Economics.

Ryan estimated retail sales volumes rose 0.8% from a month earlier in August, and are on track for 1.2% annualized growth in the third quarter and would be consistent with a modest slowdown in consumption growth. "Given falling employment and a rising jobless rate, that would not be considered a bad result."

Cardholder tracking by Royal Bank of Canada indicates spending by its customers is slowing. Spending on items excluding cars and gasoline grew 0.4% in August and has slowed three months running based on a three-month moving average, a trend Royal Bank economist Rachel Battaglia said aligns with expectations that Canada's economy is growing slowly and contracting sharply in the second quarter.

Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem has cautioned that low population growth and weakness in the labor market will likely weigh on household spending in the months ahead. The central bank this week lowered its policy interest rate by a quarter percentage point, the first cut since March, as inflation risks have ebbed and after the economy lost roughly more than 100,000 jobs over July and August.

The fall in retail sales in July was seen across a broad range of goods, with Canadians spending less on groceries and on clothing in particular after a lift in both categories the month before.

Automotive sales was the one positive for the month, nudging up on strength at "other" vehicle dealers, which covers autos including recreational vehicles and motorcycles. That left retail sales excluding the autos sector down 1.2% month-on-month.

In volume terms, sales in July was 0.8% lower.

"While the July retail sales figures were soft, a decent August suggests Canadian consumers didn't stay down for long. Despite ongoing trade uncertainty and further weakening in the labor market, the economy looks to be on track for a modest recovery to start the third quarter," said Shelly Kaushik, senior economist at Bank of Montreal Capital Markets.

Economists expect the weakness in trade-exposed sectors of Canada will continue to broaden and for the unemployment rate to continue edging upward, which in turn could dampen Canadians' shopping. Katherine Judge, senior economist at CIBC Capital Markets, said it also is likely a "buy Canadian" movement in response to U.S. tariffs fades over the winter as domestic travel wanes, which will put further pressure on consumer spending.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: ICE Canola Midday: Finding Traction to the Downside
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange were lower late Friday morning, after starting the day trading either side of unchanged.

"I don't see anything that will move stuff dramatically," an analyst said.

Losses in Chicago soybeans and soyoil plus those in European rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil pressured canola. Modest declines in crude oil weighed on the vegetable oils.

The November canola contract remained behind its 20-day moving average. Crush margins continued to recede with the November positions between C$185 and C$193 per metric ton above the futures.

Despite the slow pace of the Prairie harvest, it was picking up.

Saskatchewan reported its overall harvest was over the halfway mark with the province's canola 21 per cent completed.

While the western Prairies are expected to remain dry through the weekend, parts of the eastern half are to get rain particularly southern Manitoba.

The Canadian dollar was slightly higher by mid-session Friday, with the loonie at 72.55 U.S. cents compared to Thursday's close of 72.48.

Approximately 24,300 canola contracts were traded as of 11:24 a.m. EDT, with prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton:

 
           Price      Change 
Nov       620.30     dn 3.60 
Jan       633.50     dn 2.90 
Mar       644.70     dn 2.90 
May       654.20     dn 3.20

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.description:
RNS Number : 1198A Caledonia Investments PLC 19 September 2025  

Caledonia Investments plc

Notification of transactions of persons discharging managerial responsibilities ("PDMR") or persons closely associated with them ("PCA")

Caledonia Investments plc ("Caledonia" or the "Company") has been notified pursuant to the requirements of the UK Market Abuse Regulation ("MAR") of the following transactions in its ordinary shares of 0.5p ("Shares") involving one of its directors, William Wyatt, and a person closely associated with him.

On 17 September 2025, 92,050 Shares were transferred to Mr Wyatt under the Caledonia Investments 2020 Performance Share Scheme ("PSS") at nil cost. The Shares were sourced from The Caledonia Investments plc Employee Share Trust ("EST"). This transaction took place off-market in London.

On the same date, Mr Wyatt sold all 92,050 Shares to The Dunchurch Lodge Stud Company ("Dunchurch Lodge"), a private company controlled by him and persons connected with him. The Shares were sold at 376.77p per Share and Dunchurch Lodge purchased the Shares at 376.8p per Share.

On the same date, Dunchurch Lodge purchased the following Shares from another member of the Cayzer family concert party:

·    5,500 Shares at 376.8p per Share; and

·    1,000 Shares at 377.5384p per Share.

Each of these transactions were conducted on-market in London and increased Mr Wyatt's beneficial interests in the Company.

Mr Wyatt's total interests in Shares following the above transactions are as follows:

** includes 10,772,640 Shares owned by Dunchurch Lodge and 101,350 Shares owned by Knossington Holdings Company.

data.title: ‘Huge category’: Grayscale officially launches GDLC, the first index-based spot crypto ETF
data.description:
Crypto asset manager Grayscale has officially launched its mixed-crypto exchange-traded product, the Grayscale CoinDesk Crypto 5 ETF (ticker GDLC), the first multi-asset crypto ETP in the United States.

GDLC offers exposure to the broader crypto asset class while focusing on the current market leaders BTC, ETH, SOL, XRP, and ADA. Bitcoin accounts for about 73% of the fund's weight, followed by Ethereum (17%), according to its website.

"This launch is more than just another ETP – it’s a reflection of our decade-long commitment to being first, moving fast, and giving investors transparent exposure to the crypto ecosystem," Grayscale CEO Peter Mintzberg said in a post on X.

The index was developed by CoinDesk Markets and will automatically be rebalanced every quarter to "ensure alignment with the evolving market leadership in crypto."

"IMO, index-based & actively managed spot crypto ETFs will be met w/ *significant* demand, esp from financial advisors," NovaDius Wealth Management President Nate Geraci said on X. "Will be *huge* category."

Back in July, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission approved to convert the Grayscale Digital Large Cap Fund LLC on an "accelerated basis" but hit pause just one day later. Since then, under new SEC Chair Paul Atkins, the agency has further softened and changed its stance on a host of issues in the crypto space. Most notably, the agency's "Project Crypto" initiative seeks to modernize the agency's existing rules around digital assets.

Just this week, the SEC approved new exchange listing standards, which could fast-track crypto ETF listings. With nearly 100 crypto-related ETF proposals awaiting approval, Bloomberg senior ETF analyst Eric Balchunas says there’s a "good chance" over 100 crypto ETFs will be launched in the next 12 months.

Of note, the first U.S. ETFs to offer spot exposure to XRP and Dogecoin started trading on Thursday and generated about $55 million in trading volume.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

© 2025 The Block. All Rights Reserved. This article is provided for informational purposes only. It is not offered or intended to be used as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice.

data.title: Plug Power Stock Is On A Tear. What Gives? — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Shares of hydrogen technology provider Plug Power are rising, again. Exactly why is hard to figure out.

It could be an old-fashioned short squeeze.

Shares of the hydrogen technology provider traded as high as $2.30 before sliding back to $2.18, up 3.6%, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 0.2%.

It's the eighth consecutive rise for shares, continuing a rally that has moved the stock up a surprising 55%.

There haven't been any upgrades, price target changes, or material company updates. The average analyst price target is unchanged over the past week at about $2 a share.

Shares surged 19% on Wednesday, following the Federal Reserve's cut of short-term rates by 0.25 percentage points, as expected, to a range of 4% to 4.25%.

Lower rates help a host of companies, reducing the cost to do business. Plug Power, for its part, doesn't generate positive operating profit or free cash flow yet and still needs external financing.

Plug is also a heavily shorted stock, with about 40% of the shares available for trading sold short. Short sellers borrow stock they don't own and sell it, betting prices will decline. In comparison, average short interest for a stock in the S&P 500 is about 3%, according to FactSet.

High short interest can turbocharge any stock rally, with investors rushing to cover bearish bets to minimize losses if the stock rises. That's called a short squeeze on Wall Street.

That's one possible explanation for what's going on with Plug stock.

Coming into Friday trading, Plug stock was down about 1% year to date. Its share price has ranged from 69 cents to $3.32 over the past 12 months.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
